nativeRange.habitat: Sandy and muddy bottoms at depths of approximately 29–230 m; demersal marine environment

diet.notes: Has been documented eating small crustaceans, polychaete worms, small fish, clams, and zoobenthos in the wild. In aquaria, generally advised to start with live saltwater feeder shrimp to entice feeding; may eventually accept frozen shrimp, krill, and similar meaty foods with target feeding. Has been known to refuse food when first introduced.

avoidWith: small invertebrates such as shrimp and crabs

summary: An unusual bottom-dwelling batfish from the Western Atlantic that creeps along sandy substrates using modified pectoral and pelvic fins. Generally considered challenging to keep due to its specialized feeding needs.
careNotes:
The Longnose Batfish (Ogcocephalus corniger) is a highly distinctive, dorsoventrally flattened fish that tends to walk or crawl along the sea floor using its strong pectoral and pelvic fins rather than swim. It has a characteristic elongated rostral projection (a moveable antenna-like lure) used to attract prey. In an aquarium, it generally requires a deep sand or mud substrate, dim lighting, and low water flow to feel at ease.

Feeding is commonly the greatest challenge with this species. When first introduced, live saltwater feeder shrimp are generally advised to initiate a feeding response; target feeding with a stick or turkey baster is often recommended to ensure the batfish gets enough food without competition. Some individuals have been known to transition to frozen meaty foods over time, while others may continue to require live prey.

This fish tends to be peaceful toward tankmates it cannot eat, but is an ambush predator that will attempt to consume smaller fish and invertebrates. Tankmates must be chosen carefully — fast-moving, aggressive feeders will outcompete it; very small fish or invertebrates may be consumed. Sources disagree on reef safety: LiveAquaria lists it as reef-compatible, while other sources note it tends to knock over coral structures and consume small invertebrates. 'With caution' is the most prudent classification, and individual behavior may vary.

Care level disagreement: LiveAquaria rates this species as 'Difficult' (mapped to advanced), while SaltwaterFish.com rates it 'Expert'. The primary challenge is feeding, not water quality sensitivity per se. 'Advanced' has been selected as the averaged assessment.

Tank size disagreement: LiveAquaria and TankFacts recommend a 70-gallon (265 L) minimum; SaltwaterFish.com recommends 150 gallons (568 L). Given the >2× spread and the fish's behavioral needs (open sandy bottom, tendency to disturb rockwork), 400 L has been selected as a cautious round-number recommendation.

Adult size: Most sources (FishBase, LiveAquaria, Reeflex) converge around 23–24 cm TL. One outlier (SaltwaterFish.com) states 30 cm (13 inches), which may reflect a different measurement convention or source error; the 23–24 cm figure from FishBase is treated as more authoritative.

Temperature: Averaged across three aquarium-care sources (LiveAquaria: 22–26°C, TankFacts: 24–27°C, SaltwaterFish: 22–26°C). FishBase reports a broader wild range of 18–27°C. An aquarium range of 23–26°C is considered reasonable.

The species has been observed jumping and crawling out of water; a tightly fitted lid is advisable. Reeflex reports no confirmed captive keeping records for this species, suggesting captive maintenance is uncommon and there is limited published husbandry data.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been documented. Very limited information is available on reproductive behavior. Reeflex reports no captive keeping records for this species.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (23.0 cm TL), taxonomy, native range, and wild temperature range.

additional.name: TankFacts
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (76–80°F / 24–27°C), pH, salinity, adult size (10 in / 25.4 cm). Tank size agreed with LiveAquaria at 70 gal.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com
additional.notes: Provided additional cross-check. Disagreed on tank size (150 gal / 568 L vs 70 gal / 265 L from other sources), care level (Expert vs Difficult), reef safety (No vs Yes), and adult size (30 cm vs ~23 cm). These disagreements drove needs_review status.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, native range (Cuba, Gulf of Mexico, Bahamas, USA), adult size (23 cm), and temperature (25–28°C from FishBase data). No confirmed captive keeping records reported.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented. Very limited information available on reproductive behavior in the wild or aquarium.
